DOCUMENT TYPE: requirements
DOCUMENT PATH: docs/brainstorms/2026-04-24-cairn-systematic-review-requirements.md

[Document content: a multi-persona parallel review of cairn 0.6.0. Goals: identify P0/P1/P2 findings, empirically verify injection hook, cross-check schema. Approach: 6 concurrent agents (2 deep lens: injection-correctness, frontmatter/Obsidian-schema; 4 breadth: correctness, reliability, architecture, CLI-agent-readiness) + synthesis pass + manual Obsidian loop. Deliverable: docs/reviews/2026-04-24-cairn-systematic-review.md. Success criteria: every churn area has a persona; schema cross-reference table; injection hook exercised against ≥7 vault states; findings deduped; severity justified. Non-goals include effectiveness measurement, Obsidian parser implementation. Open questions: persist test fixtures? threat model depth? test review scope? Notes for planning: 6 agents have unequal weight; lens agents need more setup.]

Read full doc at the path above. Look for: scope/goal misalignment, unnecessary ceremony, premature abstractions, multiple priority tiers without justification, framework-building when bespoke would suffice.

content.text:
Now I have a complete picture. The existing inject test covers 5 of the document's 7 required vault states (missing vault = "empty vault" variant; the 50-session budget test covers "30+ sessions"; the cached summary test covers "sessions with cached summary"; priority ordering is covered). The two missing states from the document's list that the existing tests don't cover are: (1) pathological content (backslashes, double quotes, embedded `---`, CJK/emoji, CRLF, 50KB manifest) and (2) `context.md` alone exceeding 2KB. This is load-bearing for my scope analysis.

```json
{
  "reviewer": "scope-guardian",
  "findings": [
    {
      "title": "Live injection exercise duplicates 4 of 5 existing test cases",
      "severity": "P1",
      "section": "Approach — Deep lens agents — Injection correctness lens — Live exercise",
      "why_it_matters": "Agents 1 would re-run 'empty vault', '30+ sessions budget', 'priority ordering', and 'cached summary preference' scenarios that tests/inject.test.ts already exercises. Paying synthesis cost and agent wall-clock time to rediscover what existing Vitest tests already assert is waste, not coverage. The net-new surface is exactly two scenarios: pathological content (escape sequences, CJK, CRLF, embedded YAML delimiters) and context.md-alone-exceeds-budget. Scoping the live exercise to only those two would cut the injection lens setup time by ~60% while preserving full marginal value.",
      "finding_type": "error",
      "autofix_class": "present",
      "confidence": 0.92,
      "evidence": [
        "Existing tests/inject.test.ts line 48: 'should exit 0 with empty context when vault missing' — covers document's 'empty vault' state",
        "Line 61: 'should respect 2KB budget' — spawns 50 session files, covers document's 'vault with 30+ sessions'",
        "Line 86: 'should inject context.md before index.md' — covers document's priority-ordering invariant",
        "Line 104: 'should prefer cached session summaries' — covers document's 'with and without cached summary' state",
        "Document live exercise list: 'empty vault / vault with only context.md / vault with all four top-level files but context.md exceeds 2KB on its own / vault with 30+ sessions, each with and without cached summary / vault containing pathological content / vault with entire available and unavailable'"
      ],
      "suggested_fix": "Narrow the injection lens live exercise to the two scenarios not covered by existing tests: (a) pathological vault content (escape chars, CJK, CRLF, embedded ---) and (b) context.md alone exceeding BUDGET. Replace the full seven-state table with a delta table: 'covered by existing tests / covered by live exercise / not exercised'."
    },
    {
      "title": "Agent #3 correctness reviewer embeds a second full persona (project-standards-reviewer)",
      "severity": "P2",
      "section": "Approach — Breadth persona agents — agent #3",
      "why_it_matters": "The document assigns agent #3 both correctness review and a full project-standards pass against the global CLAUDE.md. These are distinct workloads with different scopes (bug-hunting vs. style/architecture conformance). Bundling them into one agent risks one concern crowding out the other in the synthesis input, and it undermines the stated rationale for six concurrent agents (independent parallel lenses). Either give the standards pass its own agent slot or explicitly acknowledge it is subordinate and bounded (e.g., 'flag only P2 or higher standards violations as incidental findings, not a full audit').",
      "finding_type": "omission",
      "autofix_class": "present",
      "confidence": 0.82,
      "evidence": [
        "Document text: 'A correctness-and-pattern check against the project standards: project-standards-reviewer comparing the code against the user's global CLAUDE.md (TypeScript strict, no any, layered architecture, Pino-equivalent structured logging, etc.) — invoked as part of agent #3.'",
        "Document rationale for six agents: 'Hybrid parallel review, weighted to the prioritized dimensions.' No rationale given for collapsing standards review into agent #3 rather than allocating it a breadth slot."
      ],
      "suggested_fix": "Either (a) give the standards check its own breadth slot (making seven agents total, justify the addition), or (b) explicitly cap it: 'Agent #3 flags standards violations only when they coincide with a correctness or reliability concern — not as a standalone audit.'"
    },
    {
      "title": "Seven-state tabulated results table is output structure, not a new abstraction — no scope issue",
      "severity": "P3",
      "section": "Deliverable — Cross-references — Synthetic-vault test results table",
      "why_it_matters": "The requirement to tabulate injection hook results across vault states is proportional to the two net-new pathological-content scenarios. The table format itself is lightweight and has a direct consumer (the synthesis agent merging into the review doc). This is noted only because if finding #1 is accepted and the live exercise is narrowed to two scenarios, the table shrinks to two rows, making the schema-cross-reference table the more substantive cross-reference deliverable.",
      "finding_type": "omission",
      "autofix_class": "present",
      "confidence": 0.68,
      "evidence": [
        "Document deliverable spec: 'Cross-references: Schema cross-reference table (frontmatter lens deliverable). Synthetic-vault test results table (injection lens deliverable).'",
        "Existing tests cover 4 of 7 listed states, leaving 2 net-new (pathological content; context.md-exceeds-budget alone) — the table would be mostly redundant without the scope narrowing."
      ],
      "suggested_fix": "If finding #1 is accepted: rename to 'Injection lens delta table' and scope it to the two net-new scenarios only. If the full seven states are kept, annotate each row with 'covered by existing test / exercised live' to avoid re-reporting known behavior."
    },
    {
      "title": "Open question Q1 (persist fixtures) deferred without noting it blocks regression value",
      "severity": "P2",
      "section": "Open Questions — Q1",
      "why_it_matters": "Q1 asks whether scratch-vault outputs persist as test fixtures. The default answer is 'ephemeral.' But the two scenarios the live exercise uniquely covers (pathological content, context.md-overbudget) are exactly the edge cases most likely to regress. An ephemeral exercise produces findings but no regression guard — the next code change to hooks/inject could silently re-introduce them. The document does not call out this asymmetry, which means the planning phase may close Q1 as 'ephemeral' without understanding the trade-off. Noting this gap does not require changing the default; it requires the planning artifact to record the consequence.",
      "finding_type": "omission",
      "autofix_class": "present",
      "confidence": 0.78,
      "evidence": [
        "Document Q1: 'Should the live exercise of the inject hook persist its scratch-vault outputs as fixtures in tests/? Default: ephemeral. Persisting them is a planning-phase decision tied to whether we want regression tests against pathological vault content.'",
        "Existing inject test already fixtures normal vault states — only the pathological states would be net-new fixtures. The cost of persisting two fixture files is low; the cost of not doing so is re-discovering the same bugs after the next hook change."
      ],
      "suggested_fix": "Add to Q1: 'Note: the two net-new states (pathological content, context.md-alone-overbudget) have no current regression guard. If the live exercise reveals bugs, persisting those fixtures as two new test cases in tests/inject.test.ts is recommended regardless of the default answer here.'"
    },
    {
      "title": "Synthesis pass receives unspecified 'structured per-agent output' with no shape contract",
      "severity": "P2",
      "section": "Notes for Planning",
      "why_it_matters": "The document states 'Plan for the synthesis pass to receive structured per-agent output (markdown sections), not just text — a small shape spec will help merging.' This acknowledges the gap but stops short of defining the shape. Without a concrete structure (e.g., required fields per finding: title, file:line, severity, what-fails, suggested-follow-up), each of the six agents will produce differently structured output and the synthesis pass becomes a reformatting task rather than a merge task. The document already specifies exactly these fields in the Deliverable section — they should be surfaced in the Notes for Planning as the required output schema each agent must emit, not left as a planning-phase concern.",
      "finding_type": "omission",
      "autofix_class": "present",
      "confidence": 0.85,
      "evidence": [
        "Notes for Planning: 'Plan for the synthesis pass to receive structured per-agent output (markdown sections), not just text — a small shape spec will help merging.'",
        "Deliverable section already specifies: 'Each finding includes: title, file path with line range, severity, what's wrong, what fails as a result, suggested follow-up.'",
        "The shape is defined in one section and not referenced from the other — the requirement exists but the connection is not made explicit, risking six agents producing six different schemas."
      ],
      "suggested_fix": "In Notes for Planning, add: 'Each agent emits findings using the schema defined in the Deliverable section: title | file:line | severity | what's wrong | what fails | suggested follow-up. The synthesis pass merges, dedupes, and ranks — it does not reformat.'"
    }
  ],
  "residual_risks": [
    "The seven-agent fan-out (six concurrent + one synthesis) is proportionate to a 2,600 LOC solo-dev codebase only if agent scope boundaries are tight. Finding #2 (agent #3 double-load) and the underspecified synthesis schema (finding #5) are the two places most likely to cause the synthesis pass to become the actual bottleneck — requiring a second synthesis iteration that the plan does not account for.",
    "The 'verify in Obsidian' manual loop is correctly called out as non-automated, but the document gives it no time-box or exit criteria. Without an explicit checklist of pass/fail conditions, the manual loop can expand unboundedly or be skipped under time pressure."
  ],
  "deferred_questions": [
    "The document lists both 'entire_checkpoint IS hashed today' as a manifest-hash-coverage observation and flags it as a potential design question (should a checkpoint addition mid-session invalidate a summary?). This is a correctness question about the hash invariant design, not just a documentation drift item — should it be escalated to P1 in the frontmatter lens deliverable if the answer is 'no, it should not invalidate'?",
    "Agent #6 (CLI agent readiness) is asked to evaluate 'cairn summarize --json' output stability, but the document does not specify whether --json mode currently exists or is a gap. If it is a gap, agent #6 findings would include a missing feature rather than a behavior audit — is that in scope for the review, or does it belong in a follow-on planning document?"
  ]
}
```

OUTPUT CONTRACT: ONLY valid JSON.
{"reviewer":"adversarial","findings":[...],"residual_risks":[...],"deferred_questions":[...]}

Each finding: title (≤10 words), severity (P0-P3), section, why_it_matters, finding_type (error|omission), autofix_class (auto|present), confidence (0-1), evidence (≥1 quotes). suggested_fix when auto.

Rules: leaf reviewer; read-only; stay in adversarial lane; empty findings array OK.

DOCUMENT TYPE: requirements
DOCUMENT PATH: docs/brainstorms/2026-04-24-cairn-systematic-review-requirements.md

[Doc proposes 6-agent parallel review (2 deep lens for injection-correctness + frontmatter/Obsidian schema; 4 breadth: correctness, reliability, architecture, CLI-agent-readiness) + synthesis + manual Obsidian loop. Goals span correctness, design, reliability, security, agent-behavior alignment. Non-goals: implementation fixes, full Obsidian parser, multi-week dogfooding effectiveness measurement. Output: docs/reviews/2026-04-24-cairn-systematic-review.md with severity-ordered findings.]

Read full doc at the path above. Look for: unstated assumptions, premises that don't hold, decisions made for weak reasons, alternative framings the doc dismisses or never considers.

Without a tie-breaker rule, the synthesis pass either silently drops valid findings or produces near-duplicates with conflicting severity. (further overlap with architecture strategist)"],"suggested_fix":"Define a dedup protocol: (a) findings keyed by (file, line-range, behavior-claimed); (b) on collision keep highest severity and merge evidence; (c) require synthesis to log dropped duplicates so the dedup decision is auditable, not invisible."},{"title":"Six concurrent agents assumed independent","severity":"P2","section":"Approach + Notes for Planning","why_it_matters":"The plan asserts 'fan-out is six concurrent agents' but several agents share scope (injection lens vs correctness vs reliability all touch hooks/inject and lockfile; frontmatter lens vs architecture strategist both reason about manifest.ts boundaries). In practice, three agents independently reading the same bash script tend to reproduce the same surface-level observations and miss the same blind spots — parallel != diverse. Make 'no findings' an acceptable, complete output."},{"title":"'Does Claude actually behave the way templates assume' is unanswerable by static review","severity":"P1","section":"Problem Frame + Goal 1 (agent-behavior alignment) + Persona 6","why_it_matters":"The problem frame explicitly asks 'does Claude actually behave the way the templates assume?' and goal 1 lists 'agent-behavior alignment' as a review dimension. But none of the six personas actually run Claude against the templates. Apply the adversarial-document-reviewer persona — challenge premises, surface unstated assumptions, stress-test decisions. Do NOT evaluate document quality; instead try to break the proposed approach.

OUTPUT CONTRACT: ONLY valid JSON.
{"reviewer":"adversarial","findings":[...],"residual_risks":[...],"deferred_questions":[...]}

Each finding: title (≤10 words), severity (P0-P3), section, why_it_matters, finding_type (error|omission), autofix_class (auto|present), confidence (0-1), evidence (≥1 quotes). suggested_fix when auto.

Rules: leaf reviewer; read-only; stay in adversarial lane; empty findings array OK.

Cairn repo: /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/cairn — small Claude Code plugin, persistent markdown vault, ~2,600 LOC, solo dev, 0.6.0.

DOCUMENT TYPE: requirements
DOCUMENT PATH: docs/brainstorms/2026-04-24-cairn-systematic-review-requirements.md

[Doc proposes 6-agent parallel review (2 deep lens for injection-correctness + frontmatter/Obsidian schema; 4 breadth: correctness, reliability, architecture, CLI-agent-readiness) + synthesis + manual Obsidian loop. Goals span correctness, design, reliability, security, agent-behavior alignment. Non-goals: implementation fixes, full Obsidian parser, multi-week dogfooding effectiveness measurement. Output: docs/reviews/2026-04-24-cairn-systematic-review.md with severity-ordered findings.]

Read full doc at the path above. Look for: unstated assumptions, premises that don't hold, decisions made for weak reasons, alternative framings the doc dismisses or never considers.

lastPrompt: /compound-engineering:ce-brainstorm I want to do a systematic review of this implementation, how it works, its obsidian frontmatter formaatting, how it injects context, how much context, etc

content.text: All six personas returned. Loading the synthesis pipeline reference.

content.content:
1	# Phases 3-5: Synthesis, Presentation, and Next Action
2	
3	## Phase 3: Synthesize Findings
4	
5	Process findings from all agents through this pipeline. **Order matters** -- each step depends on the previous.
6	
7	### 3.1 Validate
8	
9	Check each agent's returned JSON against the findings schema:
10	- Drop findings missing any required field defined in the schema
11	- Drop findings with invalid enum values
12	- Note the agent name for any malformed output in the Coverage section
13	
14	### 3.2 Confidence Gate
15	
16	Suppress findings below 0.50 confidence. Store them as residual concerns for potential promotion in step 3.4.
17	
18	### 3.3 Deduplicate
19	
20	Fingerprint each finding using `normalize(section) + normalize(title)`. Normalization: lowercase, strip punctuation, collapse whitespace.
21	
22	When fingerprints match across personas:
23	- If the findings recommend **opposing actions** (e.g., one says cut, the other says keep), do not merge -- preserve both for contradiction resolution in 3.5
24	- Otherwise merge: keep the highest severity, keep the highest confidence, union all evidence arrays, note all agreeing reviewers (e.g., "coherence, feasibility")
25	- **Coverage attribution:** Attribute the merged finding to the persona with the highest confidence. Decrement the losing persona's Findings count *and* the corresponding route bucket (Auto or Present) so `Findings = Auto + Present` stays exact.
26	
27	### 3.4 Promote Residual Concerns
28	
29	Scan the residual concerns (findings suppressed in 3.2) for:
30	- **Cross-persona corroboration**: A residual concern from Persona A overlaps with an above-threshold finding from Persona B. Promote at P2 with confidence 0.55-0.65. Inherit `finding_type` from the corroborating above-threshold finding.
31	- **Concrete blocking risks**: A residual concern describes a specific, concrete risk that would block implementation. Promote at P2 with confidence 0.55. Set `finding_type: omission` (blocking risks surfaced as residual concerns are inherently about something the document failed to address).
32	
33	### 3.5 Resolve Contradictions
34	
35	When personas disagree on the same section:
36	- Create a **combined finding** presenting both perspectives
37	- Set `autofix_class: present`
38	- Set `finding_type: error` (contradictions are by definition about conflicting things the document says, not things it omits)
39	- Frame as a tradeoff, not a verdict
40	
41	Specific conflict patterns:
42	- Coherence says "keep for consistency" + scope-guardian says "cut for simplicity" -> combined finding, let user decide
43	- Feasibility says "this is impossible" + product-lens says "this is essential" -> P1 finding framed as a tradeoff
44	- Multiple personas flag the same issue -> merge into single finding, note consensus, increase confidence
45	
46	### 3.6 Promote Pattern-Resolved Findings
47	
48	Scan `present` findings for codebase-pattern-resolved auto-eligibility. Promote `present` -> `auto` when **all three** conditions are met:
49	
50	1. The finding's `why_it_matters` cites a specific existing codebase pattern -- not just "best practice" or "convention," but a concrete pattern with a file, function, or usage reference
51	2. The finding includes a concrete `suggested_fix` that follows that cited pattern
52	3. There is no genuine tradeoff -- the codebase context resolves any ambiguity about which approach to use
53	
54	The principle: when a reviewer mentions multiple theoretical approaches but the codebase already has an established pattern that makes one approach clearly correct, the codebase context settles the question. Alternatives mentioned in passing do not create a real tradeoff if the evidence shows the codebase has already chosen.
55	
56	Additional auto-promotion patterns (promote `present` -> `auto` when):
57	- The finding identifies factually incorrect behavior in the document and the suggested fix describes the correct behavior (not a design choice between alternatives)
58	- The finding identifies a missing industry-standard security control where the document's own context makes the omission clearly wrong (not a legitimate design choice for the system described), and the suggested fix follows established practice
59	- The finding identifies an incomplete technical description and the complete version is directly derivable from the codebase (the reviewer cited specific code showing what the description should say)
60	
61	Do not promote if the finding involves scope or priority changes where the document author may have weighed tradeoffs invisible to the reviewer.
62	
63	### 3.7 Route by Autofix Class
64	
65	**Severity and autofix_class are independent.** A P1 finding can be `auto` if the correct fix is obvious. The test is not "how important?" but "is there one clear correct fix, or does this require judgment?"
66	
67	| Autofix Class | Route |
68	|---------------|-------|
69	| `auto` | Apply automatically -- one clear correct fix. Includes internal reconciliation (one part authoritative over another), additions mechanically implied by the document's own content, and codebase-pattern-resolved fixes where codebase evidence makes one approach clearly correct. |
70	| `present` | Present individually for user judgment |
71	
72	Demote any `auto` finding that lacks a `suggested_fix` to `present`.
73	
74	**Auto-eligible patterns:** summary/detail mismatch (body is authoritative over overview), wrong counts, missing list entries derivable from elsewhere in the document, stale internal cross-references, terminology drift, prose/diagram contradictions where prose is more detailed, missing steps mechanically implied by other content, unstated thresholds implied by surrounding context, completeness gaps where the correct addition is obvious, codebase-pattern-resolved fixes where the reviewer cites a specific existing pattern and the suggested_fix follows it, factually incorrect behavior where the correct behavior is obvious from context or the codebase, missing standard security controls with known implementations, incomplete technical descriptions where the complete version is derivable from the codebase. If the fix requires judgment about *what* to do (not just *what to write*) and the codebase context does not resolve the ambiguity, it belongs in `present`.
75	
76	### 3.8 Sort
77	
78	Sort findings for presentation: P0 -> P1 -> P2 -> P3, then by finding type (errors before omissions), then by confidence (descending), then by document order (section position).
79	
80	## Phase 4: Apply and Present
81	
82	### Apply Auto-fixes
83	
84	Apply all `auto` findings to the document in a **single pass**:
85	- Edit the document inline using the platform's edit tool
86	- Track what was changed for the "Auto-fixes Applied" section
87	- Do not ask for approval -- these have one clear correct fix
88	
89	List every auto-fix in the output summary so the user can see what changed. Use enough detail to convey the substance of each fix (section, what was changed, reviewer attribution). This is especially important for fixes that add content or touch document meaning -- the user should not have to diff the document to understand what the review did.
90	
91	### Present Remaining Findings
92	
93	**Headless mode:** Do not use interactive question tools. Output all non-auto findings as a structured text summary the caller can parse and act on:
94	
95	```
96	Document review complete (headless mode).
97	
98	Applied N auto-fixes:
99	- <section>: <what was changed> (<reviewer>)
100	- <section>: <what was changed> (<reviewer>)
101	
102	Findings (requires judgment):
103	
104	[P0] Section: <section> — <title> (<reviewer>, confidence <N>)
105	  Why: <why_it_matters>
106	  Suggested fix: <suggested_fix or "none">
107	
108	[P1] Section: <section> — <title> (<reviewer>, confidence <N>)
109	  Why: <why_it_matters>
110	  Suggested fix: <suggested_fix or "none">
111	
112	Residual concerns:
113	- <concern> (<source>)
114	
115	Deferred questions:
116	- <question> (<source>)
117	```
118	
119	Omit any section with zero items. Then proceed directly to Phase 5 (which returns immediately in headless mode).
120	
121	**Interactive mode:**
122	
123	Present `present` findings using the review output template (read `references/review-output-template.md`). Within each severity level, separate findings by type:
124	- **Errors** (design tensions, contradictions, incorrect statements) first -- these need resolution
125	- **Omissions** (missing steps, absent details, forgotten entries) second -- these need additions
126	
127	Brief summary at the top: "Applied N auto-fixes. K findings to consider (X errors, Y omissions)."
128	
129	Include the Coverage table, auto-fixes applied, residual concerns, and deferred questions.
130	
131	### Protected Artifacts
132	
133	During synthesis, discard any finding that recommends deleting or removing files in:
134	- `docs/brainstorms/`
135	- `docs/plans/`
136	- `docs/solutions/`
137	
138	These are pipeline artifacts and must not be flagged for removal.
139	
140	## Phase 5: Next Action
141	
142	**Headless mode:** Return "Review complete" immediately. Do not ask questions. The caller receives the text summary from Phase 4 and handles any remaining findings.
143	
144	**Interactive mode:**
145	
146	**Ask using the platform's interactive question tool** -- do not print the question as plain text output:
147	- Claude Code: `AskUserQuestion`
148	- Codex: `request_user_input`
149	- Gemini: `ask_user`
150	- Fallback (no question tool available): present numbered options and stop; wait for the user's next message
151	
152	Offer these two options. Use the document type from Phase 1 to set the "Review complete" description:
153	
154	1. **Refine again** -- Address the findings above, then re-review
155	2. **Review complete** -- description based on document type:
156	   - requirements document: "Create technical plan with ce:plan"
157	   - plan document: "Implement with ce:work"
158	
159	After 2 refinement passes, recommend completion -- diminishing returns are likely. But if the user wants to continue, allow it.
160	
161	Return "Review complete" as the terminal signal for callers.
162	
163	## What NOT to Do
164	
165	- Do not rewrite the entire document
166	- Do not add new sections or requirements the user didn't discuss
167	- Do not over-engineer or add complexity
168	- Do not create separate review files or add metadata sections
169	- Do not modify caller skills (ce-brainstorm, ce-plan, or external plugin skills that invoke document-review)
170	
171	## Iteration Guidance
172	
173	On subsequent passes, re-dispatch personas and re-synthesize. The auto-fix mechanism and confidence gating prevent the same findings from recurring once fixed. If findings are repetitive across passes, recommend completion.

file.content:
# Phases 3-5: Synthesis, Presentation, and Next Action

## Phase 3: Synthesize Findings

Process findings from all agents through this pipeline. **Order matters** -- each step depends on the previous.

### 3.1 Validate

Check each agent's returned JSON against the findings schema:
- Drop findings missing any required field defined in the schema
- Drop findings with invalid enum values
- Note the agent name for any malformed output in the Coverage section

### 3.2 Confidence Gate

Suppress findings below 0.50 confidence. Store them as residual concerns for potential promotion in step 3.4.

### 3.3 Deduplicate

Fingerprint each finding using `normalize(section) + normalize(title)`. Normalization: lowercase, strip punctuation, collapse whitespace.

When fingerprints match across personas:
- If the findings recommend **opposing actions** (e.g., one says cut, the other says keep), do not merge -- preserve both for contradiction resolution in 3.5
- Otherwise merge: keep the highest severity, keep the highest confidence, union all evidence arrays, note all agreeing reviewers (e.g., "coherence, feasibility")
- **Coverage attribution:** Attribute the merged finding to the persona with the highest confidence. Decrement the losing persona's Findings count *and* the corresponding route bucket (Auto or Present) so `Findings = Auto + Present` stays exact.

### 3.4 Promote Residual Concerns

Scan the residual concerns (findings suppressed in 3.2) for:
- **Cross-persona corroboration**: A residual concern from Persona A overlaps with an above-threshold finding from Persona B. Promote at P2 with confidence 0.55-0.65. Inherit `finding_type` from the corroborating above-threshold finding.
- **Concrete blocking risks**: A residual concern describes a specific, concrete risk that would block implementation. Promote at P2 with confidence 0.55. Set `finding_type: omission` (blocking risks surfaced as residual concerns are inherently about something the document failed to address).

### 3.5 Resolve Contradictions

When personas disagree on the same section:
- Create a **combined finding** presenting both perspectives
- Set `autofix_class: present`
- Set `finding_type: error` (contradictions are by definition about conflicting things the document says, not things it omits)
- Frame as a tradeoff, not a verdict

Specific conflict patterns:
- Coherence says "keep for consistency" + scope-guardian says "cut for simplicity" -> combined finding, let user decide
- Feasibility says "this is impossible" + product-lens says "this is essential" -> P1 finding framed as a tradeoff
- Multiple personas flag the same issue -> merge into single finding, note consensus, increase confidence

### 3.6 Promote Pattern-Resolved Findings

Scan `present` findings for codebase-pattern-resolved auto-eligibility. Promote `present` -> `auto` when **all three** conditions are met:

1. The finding's `why_it_matters` cites a specific existing codebase pattern -- not just "best practice" or "convention," but a concrete pattern with a file, function, or usage reference
2. The finding includes a concrete `suggested_fix` that follows that cited pattern
3. There is no genuine tradeoff -- the codebase context resolves any ambiguity about which approach to use

The principle: when a reviewer mentions multiple theoretical approaches but the codebase already has an established pattern that makes one approach clearly correct, the codebase context settles the question. Alternatives mentioned in passing do not create a real tradeoff if the evidence shows the codebase has already chosen.

Additional auto-promotion patterns (promote `present` -> `auto` when):
- The finding identifies factually incorrect behavior in the document and the suggested fix describes the correct behavior (not a design choice between alternatives)
- The finding identifies a missing industry-standard security control where the document's own context makes the omission clearly wrong (not a legitimate design choice for the system described), and the suggested fix follows established practice
- The finding identifies an incomplete technical description and the complete version is directly derivable from the codebase (the reviewer cited specific code showing what the description should say)

Do not promote if the finding involves scope or priority changes where the document author may have weighed tradeoffs invisible to the reviewer.

### 3.7 Route by Autofix Class

**Severity and autofix_class are independent.** A P1 finding can be `auto` if the correct fix is obvious. The test is not "how important?" but "is there one clear correct fix, or does this require judgment?"

| Autofix Class | Route |
|---------------|-------|
| `auto` | Apply automatically -- one clear correct fix. Includes internal reconciliation (one part authoritative over another), additions mechanically implied by the document's own content, and codebase-pattern-resolved fixes where codebase evidence makes one approach clearly correct. |
| `present` | Present individually for user judgment |

Demote any `auto` finding that lacks a `suggested_fix` to `present`.

**Auto-eligible patterns:** summary/detail mismatch (body is authoritative over overview), wrong counts, missing list entries derivable from elsewhere in the document, stale internal cross-references, terminology drift, prose/diagram contradictions where prose is more detailed, missing steps mechanically implied by other content, unstated thresholds implied by surrounding context, completeness gaps where the correct addition is obvious, codebase-pattern-resolved fixes where the reviewer cites a specific existing pattern and the suggested_fix follows it, factually incorrect behavior where the correct behavior is obvious from context or the codebase, missing standard security controls with known implementations, incomplete technical descriptions where the complete version is derivable from the codebase. If the fix requires judgment about *what* to do (not just *what to write*) and the codebase context does not resolve the ambiguity, it belongs in `present`.

### 3.8 Sort

Sort findings for presentation: P0 -> P1 -> P2 -> P3, then by finding type (errors before omissions), then by confidence (descending), then by document order (section position).

## Phase 4: Apply and Present

### Apply Auto-fixes

Apply all `auto` findings to the document in a **single pass**:
- Edit the document inline using the platform's edit tool
- Track what was changed for the "Auto-fixes Applied" section
- Do not ask for approval -- these have one clear correct fix

List every auto-fix in the output summary so the user can see what changed. Use enough detail to convey the substance of each fix (section, what was changed, reviewer attribution). This is especially important for fixes that add content or touch document meaning -- the user should not have to diff the document to understand what the review did.

### Present Remaining Findings

**Headless mode:** Do not use interactive question tools. Output all non-auto findings as a structured text summary the caller can parse and act on:

```
Document review complete (headless mode).

Applied N auto-fixes:
- <section>: <what was changed> (<reviewer>)
- <section>: <what was changed> (<reviewer>)

Findings (requires judgment):

[P0] Section: <section> — <title> (<reviewer>, confidence <N>)
  Why: <why_it_matters>
  Suggested fix: <suggested_fix or "none">

[P1] Section: <section> — <title> (<reviewer>, confidence <N>)
  Why: <why_it_matters>
  Suggested fix: <suggested_fix or "none">

Residual concerns:
- <concern> (<source>)

Deferred questions:
- <question> (<source>)
```

Omit any section with zero items. Then proceed directly to Phase 5 (which returns immediately in headless mode).

**Interactive mode:**

Present `present` findings using the review output template (read `references/review-output-template.md`). Within each severity level, separate findings by type:
- **Errors** (design tensions, contradictions, incorrect statements) first -- these need resolution
- **Omissions** (missing steps, absent details, forgotten entries) second -- these need additions

Brief summary at the top: "Applied N auto-fixes. K findings to consider (X errors, Y omissions)."

Include the Coverage table, auto-fixes applied, residual concerns, and deferred questions.

### Protected Artifacts

During synthesis, discard any finding that recommends deleting or removing files in:
- `docs/brainstorms/`
- `docs/plans/`
- `docs/solutions/`

These are pipeline artifacts and must not be flagged for removal.

## Phase 5: Next Action

**Headless mode:** Return "Review complete" immediately. Do not ask questions. The caller receives the text summary from Phase 4 and handles any remaining findings.

**Interactive mode:**

**Ask using the platform's interactive question tool** -- do not print the question as plain text output:
- Claude Code: `AskUserQuestion`
- Codex: `request_user_input`
- Gemini: `ask_user`
- Fallback (no question tool available): present numbered options and stop; wait for the user's next message

Offer these two options. Use the document type from Phase 1 to set the "Review complete" description:

1. **Refine again** -- Address the findings above, then re-review
2. **Review complete** -- description based on document type:
   - requirements document: "Create technical plan with ce:plan"
   - plan document: "Implement with ce:work"

After 2 refinement passes, recommend completion -- diminishing returns are likely. But if the user wants to continue, allow it.

Return "Review complete" as the terminal signal for callers.

## What NOT to Do

- Do not rewrite the entire document
- Do not add new sections or requirements the user didn't discuss
- Do not over-engineer or add complexity
- Do not create separate review files or add metadata sections
- Do not modify caller skills (ce-brainstorm, ce-plan, or external plugin skills that invoke document-review)

## Iteration Guidance

On subsequent passes, re-dispatch personas and re-synthesize. The auto-fix mechanism and confidence gating prevent the same findings from recurring once fixed. If findings are repetitive across passes, recommend completion.

All six personas returned valid JSON. Running through the synthesis pipeline (validate → confidence-gate → dedupe → promote → route), then applying auto-fixes.

**Coverage:**
| Persona | Findings | Auto | Present |
|---|---|---|---|
| coherence | 9 | 1 | 8 |
| feasibility | 7 | 7 | 0 |
| product-lens | 7 | 7 | 0 |
| security-lens | 4 | 0 | 4 |
| scope-guardian | 5 | 0 | 5 |
| adversarial | 9 | 7 | 2 |

**Cross-persona corroboration found** (merged):
- **agent-behavior alignment goal can't be answered by static review** — product-lens P0 + adversarial P1 + product-lens P1 (Goal 1 method gap)
- **live injection exercise duplicates existing tests** — feasibility + scope-guardian (highest conf 0.92)
- **synthesis schema not contracted** — coherence + scope-guardian + adversarial
- **schema cross-check scope incomplete** — coherence + product-lens
- **Q2 prompt-injection threat model deferred without owner** — security-lens + adversarial + coherence
- **agent #3 dual-role overload** — coherence + scope-guardian
- **synthetic vault setup mechanism + read-only constraint** — feasibility + adversarial

**Promoted to auto** (codebase-pattern-resolved): live-exercise duplicates → narrow scope; synthesis schema → reference Deliverable's spec; schema cross-check scope → glob discovery.

Applying auto-fixes now.

input.old_string: Cairn 0.6.0 has shipped non-trivial recent churn — session capture refactor (manifest + lazy summarization), template/schema migration, doctor CLI, command wrappers, explicit qmd detection. The architecture is broader than any single PR review has covered: a bash injection hook, a TS CLI with seven subcommands, hook bash wrappers, three skills, five Claude Code commands, an Obsidian-flavored vault schema documented across `templates/CAIRN.md` + `skills/cairn/SKILL.md` + the TS code that writes it.
input.new_string:
Cairn 0.6.0 has shipped non-trivial recent churn — session capture refactor (manifest + lazy summarization), template/schema migration, doctor CLI, command wrappers, explicit qmd detection. The architecture is broader than any single PR review has covered: a bash injection hook, a TS CLI with seven subcommands, hook bash wrappers, three skills, five Claude Code commands, and an Obsidian-flavored vault schema asserted across `templates/CAIRN.md`, `skills/cairn/SKILL.md`, the per-page templates and rules inside CAIRN.md, `commands/*.md`, `skills/{extract,refine}/SKILL.md`, `README.md`, and the TS code that writes session manifests and summaries. Wiki-page frontmatter is never produced by code; only Claude following CAIRN.md guidance writes it.

**Triggering signals.** No incident or user bug report motivates this audit. The signals are: (a) recent churn across coupled subsystems with no whole-system pass since pre-0.5.0, (b) the schema is asserted in 8+ surfaces with no enforced consistency, (c) the inject hook is bash with hand-rolled byte counting and JSON escaping, and (d) v0.6.0 introduced a new manifest/summary boundary worth confirming under load. This is a one-time pre-1.0 sweep; future audits should be triggered by specific signals (incidents, regressions, user reports), not version-number rhythm.

input.old_string:
Scope: `hooks/inject`, `hooks/run-hook.cmd`, `hooks/hooks.json`, `tests/inject.test.ts`, the budget constant in `src/lib/constants.ts`, and the upstream code that produces what the inject hook reads (`src/commands/capture-session.ts`, `src/lib/summarizer.ts`, `templates/CAIRN.md`'s "Context injection" section).

Static review: byte-counting math (`byte_count` uses `printf %b` + `wc -c`; risk: `%b` interprets escape sequences, may double-count or undercount actual UTF-8), the `\\n` → real-newline conversion at output time vs the byte-budget computed against the literal-`\n` string, JSON escaping correctness for embedded quotes/backslashes/control chars in vault content, priority-ordering invariants when `context.md` alone exceeds the budget, the empty-context branch when no files exist, the "newest first" ordering relying on filename sort with the `YYYY-MM-DDTHH-MM-SS-<short>` format, behavior when `sessions/summaries/<name>.md` is empty or has only frontmatter (cached but body-empty), interaction with the `entire` status indicator append.

Live exercise: spawn a temporary scratch vault. Vary inputs:
- empty vault
- vault with only `context.md`
- vault with all four top-level files but `context.md` exceeds 2KB on its own
- vault with 30+ sessions, each with and without cached summary
- vault containing pathological content: backslashes, double quotes, embedded `---`, embedded literal `\n`, multibyte UTF-8 (CJK, emoji), CRLF line endings, a 50KB single session manifest
- vault with `entire` available and unavailable

For each, run the hook, capture stdout, parse the JSON, measure `additionalContext` byte length, confirm priority order, confirm escape correctness against round-trip JSON parse.

Deliverable: list of bugs with reproducer commands; list of design observations (e.g., codepoint-budget vs byte-budget mismatch given `extract.ts` slices on codepoints but the hook budgets on bytes).
input.new_string:
Scope: `hooks/inject`, `hooks/run-hook.cmd`, `hooks/hooks.json`, `tests/inject.test.ts`, the budget constant in `src/lib/constants.ts`, the codepoint-based excerpt slicer in `src/lib/excerpt.ts`, and the upstream code that produces what the inject hook reads (`src/commands/capture-session.ts`, `src/lib/summarizer.ts`, `templates/CAIRN.md`'s "Working Set" section, which describes the inject reading order).

Static review: byte-counting math (`byte_count` uses `printf %b` + `wc -c`; risk: `%b` interprets escape sequences, may double-count or undercount actual UTF-8), the `\\n` → real-newline conversion at output time vs the byte-budget computed against the literal-`\n` string, JSON escaping correctness for embedded quotes/backslashes/control chars in vault content, priority-ordering invariants when `context.md` alone exceeds the budget, the empty-context branch when no files exist, the "newest first" ordering relying on filename sort with the `YYYY-MM-DDTHH-MM-SS-<short>` format, behavior when `sessions/summaries/<name>.md` is empty or has only frontmatter (cached but body-empty), interaction with the `entire` status indicator append, and the codepoint-vs-byte boundary between excerpt extraction (`src/lib/excerpt.ts` slices on codepoints) and the hook's byte budget.

Live exercise scope: extend `tests/inject.test.ts` rather than rebuilding a parallel harness. The existing tests already cover empty vault, basic vault, 2KB budget overflow with 50+ sessions, priority ordering, and cached-summary preference. Add cases for the two net-new scenarios:

- pathological content: backslashes, double quotes, embedded `---`, embedded literal `\n`, multibyte UTF-8 (CJK, emoji), CRLF line endings, a 50KB single session manifest
- `context.md` alone exceeds budget (current tests vary session count, not single-file overflow)

Optionally add an end-to-end check: invoke the hook through Claude Code (not just bash directly) to confirm `additionalContext` reaches the model intact, since the hook contract verification only proves bash output, not what the runtime actually injects. If end-to-end is impractical, narrow the goal language from "verify behavior" to "verify the bash hook contract" and note the runtime black-box gap explicitly.

For each new case, capture stdout, parse the JSON, measure `additionalContext` byte length, confirm priority order, and confirm escape correctness against round-trip JSON parse. Tabulate results with two columns: bytes-claimed (what the hook's budget logic believed it emitted) vs bytes-actual (length of `additionalContext` after JSON decode).

Deliverable: a delta table (covered-by-existing-test / covered-by-new-test / not-exercised) plus list of bugs with reproducer commands; list of design observations.

Static review: byte-counting math (`byte_count` uses `printf %b` + `wc -c`; risk: `%b` interprets escape sequences, may double-count or undercount actual UTF-8), the `\\n` → real-newline conversion at output time vs the byte-budget computed against the literal-`\n` string, JSON escaping correctness for embedded quotes/backslashes/control chars in vault content, priority-ordering invariants when `context.md` alone exceeds the budget, the empty-context branch when no files exist, the "newest first" ordering relying on filename sort with the `YYYY-MM-DDTHH-MM-SS-<short>` format, behavior when `sessions/summaries/<name>.md` is empty or has only frontmatter (cached but body-empty), interaction with the `entire` status indicator append, and the codepoint-vs-byte boundary between excerpt extraction (`src/lib/excerpt.ts` slices on codepoints) and the hook's byte budget.

Live exercise scope: extend `tests/inject.test.ts` rather than rebuilding a parallel harness. The existing tests already cover empty vault, basic vault, 2KB budget overflow with 50+ sessions, priority ordering, and cached-summary preference. Add cases for the two net-new scenarios:

- pathological content: backslashes, double quotes, embedded `---`, embedded literal `\n`, multibyte UTF-8 (CJK, emoji), CRLF line endings, a 50KB single session manifest
- `context.md` alone exceeds budget (current tests vary session count, not single-file overflow)

Optionally add an end-to-end check: invoke the hook through Claude Code (not just bash directly) to confirm `additionalContext` reaches the model intact, since the hook contract verification only proves bash output, not what the runtime actually injects. If end-to-end is impractical, narrow the goal language from "verify behavior" to "verify the bash hook contract" and note the runtime black-box gap explicitly.

For each new case, capture stdout, parse the JSON, measure `additionalContext` byte length, confirm priority order, and confirm escape correctness against round-trip JSON parse. Tabulate results with two columns: bytes-claimed (what the hook's budget logic believed it emitted) vs bytes-actual (length of `additionalContext` after JSON decode).

Deliverable: a delta table (covered-by-existing-test / covered-by-new-test / not-exercised) plus list of bugs with reproducer commands; list of design observations.

**2. Frontmatter and Obsidian schema lens**

Scope: `src/lib/frontmatter.ts`, `src/lib/manifest.ts`, `templates/CAIRN.md` (page templates and frontmatter spec sections), `skills/cairn/SKILL.md`, `commands/*.md`, plus any ingest/query/lint/refine/extract command docs that assert a schema.

Static review:
- Schema consistency: required fields, optional fields, allowed types. Confirm `templates/CAIRN.md` ↔ `skills/cairn/SKILL.md` ↔ `src/lib/manifest.ts:SessionManifest` ↔ `src/lib/manifest.ts:SessionSummaryFrontmatter` agree. Flag any field present in one and absent in the others.
- Page-type templates in CAIRN.md: do all five (concept, entity, source-summary, comparison, overview) declare consistent required fields? `source` is required for `source-summary` per CAIRN.md but the per-type frontmatter block omits it from the example — drift?
- Wikilink syntax usage: `[[Page]]`, `[[Page|Alias]]`, `[[Page#Heading]]`, `[[Page#^block-id]]`, `![[Page]]`. Are any uses inside generated templates malformed?
- YAML serialization: `serializeFrontmatter` in `src/lib/frontmatter.ts` uses `yaml` package defaults. Verify it round-trips Obsidian-friendly forms — e.g., tags as a YAML list (`tags: [foo]` vs block style), date strings vs YAML date types, multi-line strings (transcript paths with spaces? not in practice but principle).
- Manifest hash invariance: `computeManifestHash` strips `excerpt`, `excerpt_incomplete`, `manifest_hash`, `extracted`, `tags`, `decisions`, `open_threads`, `status`. Confirm those exclusions are intentional and documented; flag any field that should be hashed but isn't (e.g., `entire_checkpoint` IS hashed today — does that match the design intent given a checkpoint addition mid-session shouldn't invalidate a summary? Or should it?).

Scope is split into two tracks because wiki-page schema and session-artifact schema have different writers:

- *Wiki-page track* (no TS writer): `templates/CAIRN.md` (page templates and frontmatter spec sections), `skills/cairn/SKILL.md`, `skills/extract/SKILL.md`, `skills/refine/SKILL.md`, `commands/ingest.md`, `commands/query.md`, `commands/lint.md`, `commands/refine.md`, `commands/extract.md`, `README.md` (page-types table). Discovery rule: enumerate every file that mentions frontmatter fields, page types, or required keys; cross-check.
- *Session-artifact track* (TS writers): `src/lib/frontmatter.ts`, `src/lib/manifest.ts`, `src/lib/summarizer.ts`, `tests/{frontmatter,manifest,summarize}.test.ts`.

Static review:
- Schema consistency: required fields, optional fields, allowed types. Confirm wiki-track surfaces agree with each other; confirm `src/lib/manifest.ts:SessionManifest` ↔ `src/lib/manifest.ts:SessionSummaryFrontmatter` agree with what `summarizer.ts` writes. Flag any field present in one surface and absent in another.
- Page-type templates in CAIRN.md: do all five (concept, entity, source-summary, comparison, overview) declare frontmatter consistent with the prose-stated requirements? Verify per-type — do not assume drift in advance.
- Wikilink syntax usage: `[[Page]]`, `[[Page|Alias]]`, `[[Page#Heading]]`, `[[Page#^block-id]]`, `![[Page]]`. Are any uses inside generated templates malformed?
- YAML serialization: `serializeFrontmatter` in `src/lib/frontmatter.ts` uses `yaml` package defaults. Verify it round-trips Obsidian-friendly forms — e.g., tags as a YAML list (`tags: [foo]` vs block style), date strings vs YAML date types, multi-line strings.
- Manifest hash invariance: `computeManifestHash` strips `excerpt`, `excerpt_incomplete`, `manifest_hash`, `extracted`, `tags`, `decisions`, `open_threads`, `status`. Confirm those exclusions are intentional and documented; flag any field that should be hashed but isn't (e.g., `entire_checkpoint` IS hashed today — does that match the design intent given a checkpoint addition mid-session shouldn't invalidate a summary? input.old_string:
**6. CLI agent readiness reviewer** — every cairn subcommand evaluated for whether an autonomous Claude can use it: discoverability (`--help` quality), output stability (JSON-parseable where it matters, e.g., `cairn summarize --json`), error messages, exit codes, idempotency cues, stdin/stdout/stderr discipline.

A correctness-and-pattern check against the project standards: project-standards-reviewer comparing the code against the user's global `CLAUDE.md` (TypeScript strict, no `any`, layered architecture, Pino-equivalent structured logging, etc.) — invoked as part of agent #3.
input.new_string:
**6. CLI agent readiness reviewer** — every cairn subcommand (`init`, `doctor`, `uninstall`, `capture-session`, `summarize`, `summaries`, `migrate-sessions`) evaluated against an explicit rubric:

1. **Stdin/argv contract** — documented in `--help`; agent can construct a valid invocation without reading source.
2. **Output stability** — stdout shape stable across runs; JSON or `--json` flag where the agent must parse (today: `cairn summarize --json` is referenced — verify it exists or surface the gap).
3. **Exit codes** — distinguish recoverable from fatal; documented.
4. **Idempotency** — claimed idempotency (e.g., `cairn init`) is testable; second run is a no-op or produces identical state.
5. **Error messages** — name the next action the agent can take, not just the failure.
6. **Stdout/stderr discipline** — diagnostic output on stderr; primary output on stdout; no interleaving that breaks parsing.

Agent 3 (correctness) also runs a bounded project-standards check against the user's global `CLAUDE.md` (TypeScript strict, no `any`, layered architecture, Pino-equivalent structured logging). Scope cap: flag standards violations only when they coincide with a correctness or reliability concern — not as a standalone audit.

After all six finish, a synthesis agent reads every finding, dedupes, ranks by severity (P0 = data loss / security / silent corruption; P1 = behavioral bug or significant drift; P2 = design observation, polish, future improvement), and writes the consolidated review doc.

input.old_string:
### Synthesis pass

After all six finish, a synthesis agent reads every finding, dedupes, ranks by severity (P0 = data loss / security / silent corruption; P1 = behavioral bug or significant drift; P2 = design observation, polish, future improvement), and writes the consolidated review doc.
input.new_string:
### Synthesis pass

After all six finish, a synthesis agent reads every finding, dedupes, ranks by severity, and writes the consolidated review doc.

**Per-agent output contract** (every agent emits findings using this shape; synthesis merges, does not reformat):

| Field | Type | Required |
|---|---|---|
| `title` | string (≤10 words) | yes |
| `file_lines` | string (e.g., `hooks/inject:88-92`) | yes |
| `severity` | `P0` \| `P1` \| `P2` \| `P3` | yes |
| `what_is_wrong` | string | yes |
| `what_fails_as_a_result` | string (the "this fails / corrupts / leaks because …" sentence) | yes for P0; recommended otherwise |
| `suggested_follow_up` | string | yes |
| `confidence` | float 0.0–1.0 | yes |
| `evidence` | array of direct quotes from code/docs | ≥1 item |

**Severity rubric:**
- `P0` — data loss / security / silent corruption. Requires a reproducer or executable trace; if the finding is purely static-reasoned, downgrade to P1.
- `P1` — behavioral bug, contract violation, or significant drift between asserted and actual behavior.
- `P2` — design observation, polish, future improvement.
- `P3` — informational note, no action required.

**Dedup protocol:**
1. Fingerprint findings by `(file_lines, behavior_claimed)`.
2. On collision: keep highest severity; merge evidence; note all agreeing reviewers; log the dropped duplicate so the dedup decision is auditable.
3. On opposing recommendations from different personas: present both as a tradeoff finding (set `present`, don't pick).

**"No findings" is a valid output for any track.** A schema-cross-reference table that confirms consistency and an injection delta table with empty bug rows are complete deliverables, not failures.

| `evidence` | array of direct quotes from code/docs | ≥1 item |

**Severity rubric:**
- `P0` — data loss / security / silent corruption. Requires a reproducer or executable trace; if the finding is purely static-reasoned, downgrade to P1.
- `P1` — behavioral bug, contract violation, or significant drift between asserted and actual behavior.
- `P2` — design observation, polish, future improvement.
- `P3` — informational note, no action required.

**Dedup protocol:**
1. Fingerprint findings by `(file_lines, behavior_claimed)`.
2. On collision: keep highest severity; merge evidence; note all agreeing reviewers; log the dropped duplicate so the dedup decision is auditable.
3. On opposing recommendations from different personas: present both as a tradeoff finding (set `present`, don't pick).

**"No findings" is a valid output for any track.** A schema-cross-reference table that confirms consistency and an injection delta table with empty bug rows are complete deliverables, not failures.

lines: -After all six finish, a synthesis agent reads every finding, dedupes, ranks by severity (P0 = data loss / security / silent corruption; P1 = behavioral bug or significant drift; P2 = design observation, polish, future improvement), and writes the consolidated review doc.
+After all six finish, a synthesis agent reads every finding, dedupes, ranks by severity, and writes the consolidated review doc.
+**Per-agent output contract** (every agent emits findings using this shape; synthesis merges, does not reformat):
+| `what_fails_as_a_result` | string (the "this fails / corrupts / leaks because …" sentence) | yes for P0; recommended otherwise |
+| `evidence` | array of direct quotes from code/docs | ≥1 item |
+- `P0` — data loss / security / silent corruption. Requires a reproducer or executable trace; if the finding is purely static-reasoned, downgrade to P1.
+- `P1` — behavioral bug, contract violation, or significant drift between asserted and actual behavior.
+2. On collision: keep highest severity; merge evidence; note all agreeing reviewers; log the dropped duplicate so the dedup decision is auditable.
+3. On opposing recommendations from different personas: present both as a tradeoff finding (set `present`, don't pick).
+**"No findings" is a valid output for any track.** A schema-cross-reference table that confirms consistency and an injection delta table with empty bug rows are complete deliverables, not failures.

A short "verify-in-Obsidian" checklist for the human reviewer: open `~/cairn` in Obsidian, confirm wikilinks resolve, frontmatter renders in the property panel, embeds inline correctly, tags surface in the tag pane. Record findings. Not automated.

## Open Questions

- **Q1.** Should the live exercise of the inject hook persist its scratch-vault outputs as fixtures in `tests/`, or is it ephemeral verification only? Default: ephemeral. Persisting them is a planning-phase decision tied to whether we want regression tests against pathological vault content.
- **Q2.** Does the review include a security threat model for the `claude -p --model haiku` summarizer subprocess (prompt injection from a vault page or a malicious manifest)? Default: yes, but only at the design level — no fuzz harness.
- **Q3.** How exhaustively does the breadth pass examine `tests/`? Default: included in scope, but findings about test quality go to P2 unless they hide a real bug.
input.new_string:
## Open Questions

- **Q1.** Should the live exercise of the inject hook persist its scratch-vault outputs as fixtures in `tests/`, or is it ephemeral verification only? Default: ephemeral. **Note:** the two net-new states (pathological content; `context.md`-alone-overbudget) have no current regression guard. If the live exercise reveals bugs, persisting those fixtures as new test cases in `tests/inject.test.ts` is recommended regardless of the default answer here.
- **Q2.** Does the review include a security threat model for the `claude -p --model haiku` summarizer subprocess (prompt injection from a vault page or a malicious manifest)? Default: yes, at the design level (no fuzz harness), **plus one empirical probe**: include a session manifest in the synthetic vault containing an "Ignore previous instructions" string, run the summarizer on it, and record whether the output contains the injected payload. Also covers `CAIRN_SUMMARIZE_COMMAND` env-override as an execution vector and `CAIRN_VAULT` path-traversal containment.
- **Q3.** How exhaustively does the breadth pass examine `tests/`? Default: included in scope, but findings about test quality go to P2 unless the test gap hides a real bug — in which case the gap inherits the bug's severity.

## Constraints

- File references in the final review use repo-relative paths (`hooks/inject`, `src/lib/manifest.ts:90`), never absolute paths.
- Personas read code; they do not modify it.
- The synthetic vault used by the injection lens lives in a temp directory and is cleaned up; no writes to `~/cairn` or the user's real vault.
- The review is a single PR-grade document, not a directory of separate reports.
input.new_string:
## Constraints

- File references in the final review use repo-relative paths (`hooks/inject`, `src/lib/manifest.ts:90`), never absolute paths.
- Personas do not modify repo source. They MAY write to a per-agent temp directory under `$TMPDIR`. They MUST NOT write to `~/cairn`, `$HOME/cairn`, or any path outside the explicit scratch directory; enforced by setting `CAIRN_VAULT` to the scratch path and verifying no writes escape it.
- The injection lens constructs its synthetic vault via direct `fs` writes (mirroring `tests/inject.test.ts`) or via `cairn init -p <tmpdir>` with `CAIRN_VAULT` overridden. The user's real vault is never touched.
- The review is a single PR-grade document, not a directory of separate reports.

content.text:
All auto-fixes applied. Surfacing remaining findings that need user judgment.

---

# Document review complete

**Applied 11 auto-fixes:**

**Problem Frame:**
- Broadened schema-defining surfaces from 3 to 8+ (added `commands/*.md`, `skills/{extract,refine}/SKILL.md`, `README.md`); clarified that wiki-page frontmatter is never written by code (feasibility, product-lens)
- Added "Triggering signals" subsection acknowledging no incident motivated this; flagged as one-time pre-1.0, not recurring cadence (product-lens)

**Injection lens:**
- Fixed nonexistent CAIRN.md "Context injection" reference → "Working Set" section (feasibility)
- Added `src/lib/excerpt.ts` to scope (it's the codepoint slicer; I had referenced "extract.ts") (coherence)
- Narrowed live exercise to two net-new scenarios (pathological content, `context.md`-alone-overbudget); existing `tests/inject.test.ts` already covers the rest. Recommend extending the test file rather than parallel harness (feasibility, scope-guardian)
- Added optional Claude-Code end-to-end check to verify the hook contract reaches the model intact (adversarial)

**Frontmatter lens:**
- Dropped fictitious "source field missing from source-summary template" claim — it's actually present in `templates/CAIRN.md:118` (feasibility)
- Split scope into wiki-page track (no TS writer) vs session-artifact track (TS writers); enumerated full surface list (feasibility, coherence, product-lens)
- Reframed "list of drift sites" → drift if any; explicit consistency confirmation otherwise (adversarial)

**CLI agent-readiness:**
- Replaced one-line description with explicit 6-criterion rubric: stdin/argv contract, output stability, exit codes, idempotency, error messages, stdout/stderr discipline (feasibility)
- Capped agent #3's project-standards check to coincident violations only (coherence, scope-guardian)

**Synthesis pass:**
- Added per-agent output schema table (8 fields) so synthesis merges, doesn't reformat
- Added explicit dedup protocol with audit log
- Stated that "no findings" is a valid output (adversarial)
- Strengthened P0 rubric: P0 requires reproducer, otherwise downgrade to P1 (adversarial)

**Deliverable:**
- Expanded the template from one-liners to full per-finding shape
- Acknowledged `docs/reviews/` is a new directory (feasibility)
- Added required "Security threat model" section to the deliverable so Q2 cannot be silently skipped (security-lens)
- Added two end-to-end behavioral scenarios to the manual checklist (Claude session uses injected context; Claude does not touch `sessions/` during lint) (product-lens)

**Q1, Q2, Constraints:**
- Q1 expanded with regression-guard trade-off note (scope-guardian)
- Q2 expanded with empirical injection probe + `CAIRN_SUMMARIZE_COMMAND` + `CAIRN_VAULT` containment (security-lens, adversarial)
- Constraints rewritten: "personas do not modify repo source; may write to per-agent tmpdir; never to `~/cairn`" (feasibility, adversarial)

---

## Findings requiring your judgment

### P0 — errors

**Audit defers the only outcome that matters** (product-lens, conf 0.82)
- *Where:* Goals + Non-Goals
- *Why:* Cairn's product thesis is "vault makes Claude more useful across sessions." Goal 1 lists "agent-behavior alignment" but Non-Goals defers "effectiveness measurement requiring real multi-week dogfooding." The review can ship clean and the product can still fail. I added two behavioral checks to the manual loop, but the deeper question stands: is a static review the highest-leverage move for a solo-dev OSS plugin, or is 1-2 weeks of dogfooding?
- *Options:* (a) accept the manual-loop additions as sufficient, keep Goal 1; (b) drop "agent-behavior alignment" from Goal 1 entirely, leaving it to a future dogfood workstream; (c) defer this audit and dogfood first.

### P1 — errors

**Six-agent parallelism unjustified for ~2,600 LOC** (product-lens + adversarial, conf 0.70)
- 4 breadth personas (correctness, reliability, architecture, CLI-readiness) overlap heavily on the same files. Could collapse to 2 lens + 1 generalist + 1 CLI-readiness = 4 agents.
- *Options:* (a) keep six; (b) drop to four; (c) two-stage pipeline: lens agents first, breadth agents seeded with their findings (adversarial's suggestion).

**`CAIRN_SUMMARIZE_COMMAND` execution vector not in any agent's scope** (security-lens, conf 0.82)
- `src/lib/summarizer.ts:335` reads the env var with no validation and passes it to `Bun.spawn`. Anyone who can write the env can run arbitrary commands during summarization.
- *Options:* (a) add to injection-correctness lens scope; (b) defer as pre-existing trust assumption (env is treated as trusted in cairn).

### P2 — selected (lower-confidence findings noted but not surfaced individually)

- **Vault-path traversal via `CAIRN_VAULT`** (security-lens, conf 0.71) — `hooks/inject:9` and `summarizer.ts:144-148` accept absolute paths without containment. Add to lens scope or accept.
- **Sensitive transcript retention** (security-lens, conf 0.75) — manifest excerpts persist transcript content; if vault is cloud-synced, excerpts leave the local machine. `.cairn/capture-errors.log` is unbounded. Add to reliability scope?
- **Six concurrent agents may not be independent** (adversarial, conf 0.65) — three breadth agents read the same files; parallel ≠ diverse. Sequential lens-first pipeline alternative.

### Residual concerns
- The "no fixes applied" rule + filed findings doc creates a risk that P0/P1s never get fixed. No follow-through mechanism is defined (separate plan? issue tracker? next sprint?).
- Synthesis pass quality depends heavily on dedupe rigor; without a tie-breaker rule, the synthesis output may be redundant-or-collapsed.

### Deferred questions (for planning phase)
- What is the success metric for the review itself? "Findings produced" is an output metric, not an outcome.
- Why is the manual Obsidian loop scoped to the human rather than a Playwright/Chrome-DevTools agent driving real Obsidian?
- Should the schema-cross-reference deliverable be a separate file given its size, or kept inline in the review doc?

---

## Phase 5: Next action

options.description: Move to technical planning. The plan will translate the requirements into concrete tasks (agent prompts, dispatch order, scratch-vault setup script, synthesis-output schema, etc.).

options.label: Refine the P0/P1 findings first
options.description: Address the P0 (audit defers effectiveness) and P1 findings (six-agent overhead, CAIRN_SUMMARIZE_COMMAND scope) interactively before planning. I'll ask follow-ups on each, then re-edit the doc.

options.label: Dogfood instead, defer audit
options.description: Park the requirements doc. Spend 1-2 weeks using cairn in real Claude Code sessions and capture what actually breaks vs what the audit would predict. Then decide if a static review is still the highest-leverage move.

options.label: Keep as-is, run the review now
options.description: Skip planning and execute the six-agent review against the current requirements doc directly. I dispatch the agents, run the synthesis pass, and write docs/reviews/2026-04-24-cairn-systematic-review.md in this session. Faster but skips a planning round.
